    xsi:schemaLocation="http://www.loc.gov/mods/v3 http://www.loc.gov/standards/mods/v3/mods-3-4.xsd" version="3.4" xmlns="http://www.loc.gov/mods/v3" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:dc="http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/" xmlns:oai_dc="http://www.openarchives.org/OAI/2.0/oai_dc/" xmlns:oai-pmh="http://www.openarchives.org/OAI/2.0/">

        </role>
      </name>
      <originInfo>
        <dateCreated keyDate="yes">May 3, 1938</dateCreated>
      </originInfo>
      <note type="content">
        A view along the boardwalk looking northwest from Beach 80th Street (now de-mapped at the boardwalk.) The boarded-up businesses include an ice cream shop, several small shops without signage, the Coffee Pot luncheonette, and the Beer Garden Restaurant at the northeast corner of Beach 81st Street. Magyary's Luncheonette, on the northwest corner, is seen at far left. The peaked-roof buildings behind the shops are rental bungalows. A view of this block taken from Beach 81st Street can be seen in record psc-000389.
      </note>

originalRecord.stringValue:
<record 
xmlns="http://www.openarchives.org/OAI/2.0/" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance">
  <header>
    <identifier>oai:repox.ist.utl.pt:collection13844:aql:17492</identifier>
    <datestamp>2018-08-08</datestamp>
    <setSpec>collection13844</setSpec>
  </header>
  <metadata>
    <mods 
    xsi:schemaLocation="http://www.loc.gov/mods/v3 http://www.loc.gov/standards/mods/v3/mods-3-4.xsd" version="3.4" xmlns="http://www.loc.gov/mods/v3" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:dc="http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/" xmlns:oai_dc="http://www.openarchives.org/OAI/2.0/oai_dc/" xmlns:oai-pmh="http://www.openarchives.org/OAI/2.0/">
      <titleInfo>
        <title>Rockaway Beach Blvd Sign</title>
      </titleInfo>
      <name>
        <namePart>Adam Zborowski</namePart>
        <role>
          <roleTerm>Creator</roleTerm>
        </role>
      </name>
      <originInfo>
        <dateCreated keyDate="yes">October 31, 2012</dateCreated>
      </originInfo>
      <note type="content">
        The Rockaways were damaged by Hurricane Sandy but survived after the storm passed. The street sign stands a a symbol of the resiliance of residents and homes that weathered the storm. [From Adam: I come from Poland, from Warsaw, soon after I finished high school I decided to go to U.S. for the first time. NYC was the place where I started my first photographic adventure. That's why at the beginning I focused mainly on urban photography, later in Poland I also photographed the industrial sites of the cities, but through those years was exploring more and more natural landscapes, and today here on Rockaway the most important for me became light, open space and atmosphere. In my art I aim to catch the place, people, situation, weather in that very moment of seeing more then usually. Before I moved to Rockaway I was living in Brooklyn for over four years. I was a witness of two biggest hurricanes in the last years, Irine and Sandy, but Sandy was the one that really changed everything around and within me. By the time of hurricane I was in Brooklyn, I came to Rockaway the day after and couldn't believe what I saw. Unfortunately I forgot to take camera with me, so next day I came there again with my camera and started document the whole disaster. I’ve been always very familiar with Rockaway and Broad Channel. Eventually I moved here two months after the hurricane, which I planned before anyway. I became a member of RAA (Rockaway Artist Alliance) and participant of three exhibitions at the gallery T7 in Fort Tilden. So far so good.]
      </note>
      <identifier>aql:17492</identifier>
      <identifier>qmp-az-000001</identifier>
      <identifier>qmp-az-000001.tif</identifier>
      <physicalDescription>
        <extent>4.6 x 8 inches; 72 dpi</extent>
        <form>Digital image</form>
      </physicalDescription>
      <subject>
        <topic>Street signs</topic>
      </subject>
      <subject>
        <topic>Hurricane Sandy, 2012</topic>
      </subject>
      <subject>
        <geographic>Rockaway Beach (New York, N.Y.)</geographic>
      </subject>
      <typeOfResource>still image</typeOfResource>
      <accessCondition>
        The Queens Library is interested in learning more about
        items you've seen on our websites or elsewhere online. If you have any more information
        about an item or its copyright status, we want to hear from you. Please contact

      </accessCondition>

        </titleInfo>
      </relatedItem>

      </location>
      <location>

        </url>
      </location>
      <note type="ownership">Queens Borough Public Library</note>
      <note type="regional council">Metropolitan New York Library Council</note>
      <relatedItem type="host" displayLabel="Collection">
        <titleInfo>
          <title>Queens Borough Public Library-Queens Memory Photographs</title>
        </titleInfo>
      </relatedItem>
</mods>

        <namePart>Christine Martinez</namePart>
        <role>

        </role>
      </name>
      <originInfo>
        <dateCreated keyDate="yes">November 3, 2012</dateCreated>
      </originInfo>
      <note type="content">
        Christine Martinez and her neighbors on Beach 91st Street set up this cooking stove on their street in the weeks after Hurricane Sandy. Their fires at night would both cook their food and also provide a gathering spot on the block where they could discuss their recovery efforts. According to Martinez, "we came up with this idea to put this stove in the middle of the street to let people know on our block that there is warmth and food available."
      </note>

      </originInfo>
      <note type="content">
        A view east along Rockaway Boulevard across the Long Island Rail Road's Rockaway Beach Division tracks, taken from a point about one hundred yards west of the track. Elimination of the grade crossing was ordered in 1927.     <mods 
    xsi:schemaLocation="http://www.loc.gov/mods/v3 http://www.loc.gov/standards/mods/v3/mods-3-4.xsd" version="3.4" xmlns="http://www.loc.gov/mods/v3" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:dc="http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/" xmlns:oai_dc="http://www.openarchives.org/OAI/2.0/oai_dc/" xmlns:oai-pmh="http://www.openarchives.org/OAI/2.0/">
      <titleInfo>
        <title>Bathing in the Breakers at Hammels</title>
      </titleInfo>
      <name>
        <namePart>Wallach, Lori</namePart>
        <role>
          <roleTerm>Creator</roleTerm>
        </role>
      </name>
      <originInfo>
        <dateCreated keyDate="yes">July 10, 1908</dateCreated>
      </originInfo>
      <note type="content">
        This postcard depicts beachgoers "bathing" in the ocean by holding on to ropes attached to poles and buoys. In the early 20th century, the Hammels community was a popular Rockaway seaside destination; it had its own stop on the Long Island Rail Road until 1941. The hand-written message on the reverse of the card reads: "Dear Cousin Olga: - You can expect Herman at 403 P.M. Sunday. Have not heard from you yet to my last letter. Your Cousin Lu." It is postmarked July 10, 1908, from Brooklyn, N.Y.
      </note>
      <note type="content">
        [Written on back] Miss Olga Schrader, Herkimer Herkimer County N.Y.; Dear Cousing Olga: You can expect Herman at 4:03 P.M. Sunday. Have not heard from you yet to my last letter. Your Cousin Lu.
      </note>
      <identifier>aql:24846</identifier>
      <identifier>qmp-wl-07062015-018</identifier>
      <identifier>qmp-wl-07062015-018.tif</identifier>
      <physicalDescription>
        <extent>5 1/2 x 3 3/8 inches; 400 dpi</extent>
        <form>Postcard</form>
      </physicalDescription>
      <subject>
        <topic>Beaches</topic>
      </subject>
      <subject>
        <topic>Swimmers</topic>
      </subject>
      <subject>
        <geographic>Herkimer (N.Y.)</geographic>
      </subject>

        </url>

description: A city health inspector notes trash dumped in a driveway of an apartment building at 23-08 Mott Avenue. The building was later ordered vacated.
relation: Queens Borough Public Library-Long Island Daily Press Photograph Morgue Collection
rights: The Queens Library is interested in learning more about items you've seen on our websites or elsewhere online. <record

  <metadata>
    <mods 
    xsi:schemaLocation="http://www.loc.gov/mods/v3 http://www.loc.gov/standards/mods/v3/mods-3-4.xsd" version="3.4" xmlns="http://www.loc.gov/mods/v3" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:dc="http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/" xmlns:oai_dc="http://www.openarchives.org/OAI/2.0/oai_dc/" xmlns:oai-pmh="http://www.openarchives.org/OAI/2.0/">
      <titleInfo>
        <title>Train at Rockaway Junction</title>
      </titleInfo>
      <name>
        <namePart>William J. Rugen</namePart>
        <role>
          <roleTerm>Creator</roleTerm>
        </role>
      </name>
      <originInfo>
        <dateCreated keyDate="yes">1896</dateCreated>
      </originInfo>
      <note type="content">
        Four men pose with Engine No. 109 and Coach No. 314 outside the Rockaway Junction and Station in what is now Jamaica. The two conductors at right are identified as William Jerome and George Oxley. The man at far left is the fireman, John Tooker, and the man looking out of the locomotive's cab is the engineer, Hugh Livingstone. The typewritten caption is part of the image on the polyester negative, and it reads, "L.I.R.R. rapid transit trains seldom consisted of more than two cars at that time (1896). There were about thirty local stations then between Flatbush Ave., Brooklyn, and Rockaway Junction, many in Brooklyn were just open platforms. Engine 109. This picture was taken at Rockaway Junction Station (now Hillside L.I.) in the summer of 1896 by an itinerant photographer named Dill. Of the four men shown, John Tooker, fireman, Hugh Livingston Engineer, William Jerome and George Oxley, Conductors, only the fireman is now living in 1953, and he is 79 years old."
      </note>
      <identifier>aql:9528</identifier>
      <identifier>wjr-000059</identifier>
      <identifier>wjr-000059.tif</identifier>
      <physicalDescription>
        <extent>
          3 1/2 x 4 1/2 (negative); 3 5/8 x 5 1/2 inches (print); 400 ppi (digital image)
        </extent>
        <form>safety film; gelatin silver print</form>
      </physicalDescription>

      <note type="content">
        A view looking southeast from North Conduit Avenue at its intersection with 101st Street. In the center background can be seen the Sunrise Highway Bridge over the Long Island Rail Road tracks. The grade-level crossing for the tracks of the Rockaway line can just be seen at the far left. Two automobiles are parked in a lot on the corner opposite. There is also a street sign, a mailbox, and a bus stop on the corner.

    <mods 
    xsi:schemaLocation="http://www.loc.gov/mods/v3 http://www.loc.gov/standards/mods/v3/mods-3-4.xsd" version="3.4" xmlns="http://www.loc.gov/mods/v3" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:dc="http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/" xmlns:oai_dc="http://www.openarchives.org/OAI/2.0/oai_dc/" xmlns:oai-pmh="http://www.openarchives.org/OAI/2.0/">
      <titleInfo>
        <title>Atlantic Avenue in Richmond Hill</title>
      </titleInfo>
      <name>
        <namePart>Public Service Commission</namePart>
        <role>
          <roleTerm>Creator</roleTerm>
        </role>
      </name>
      <originInfo>
        <dateCreated keyDate="yes">October 13, 1939</dateCreated>
      </originInfo>
      <note type="content">
        A view looking west along the north side of Atlantic Avenue from a point near 108th Street. A row of storefronts and several attached homes are at right. The Long Island Rail Road's grade-level tracks, which ran down the center of Atlantic Avenue, are beyond the fence at left. There is a pedestrian trestle on the left, which allowed passage to the other side of Atlantic Avenue. The trestle of the Long Island Rail Road's Rockaway Line, at 99th Street, is visible in the far distance.
      </note>
      <identifier>aql:4050</identifier>
      <identifier>psc-000461</identifier>
      <identifier>psc-000461.tif</identifier>
